then.verdict: The ₹1-crore-loan degree is not your only door
then.headline: At your budget and loan access, rank destinations by realistic all-in cost — several credible ones fit under ₹50L
why: Germany public universities: effectively no tuition — a semester fee of a few hundred euros. All-in cost ≈ living expenses only (~₹8–12L/yr). The catch is seat competition and German-language life outside class.
France: public universities are cheap; Grandes Écoles cost more but scholarship programs (Eiffel, Charpak) regularly cover Indian admits. Campus France is the official channel.
UK one-year master's: the sticker price looks high, but ONE year of tuition + living often totals less than two years elsewhere — shorter is cheaper.
Singapore: the Tuition Grant cuts fees sharply in exchange for a 3-year work bond in Singapore. Honest note: the bond is real and enforced — it's a commitment, not a discount.
US: a funded MS (assistantship/TA/RA) flips the math entirely — but funding is competitive and program-specific; count on it only after the offer letter says so.
Norway ended free tuition for non-EU/EEA students in 2023 — it does NOT belong on the cheap list anymore. Verify every fee claim on the university's own bursar page; agents' lists go stale.
pros: A no-loan or small-loan degree preserves the single biggest post-graduation freedom: the freedom to leave a bad job or a bad country
cons: Cheapest destinations trade off brand recognition in the US job market
Language and seat competition are real constraints in Germany/France

worst_case.scenario: You take the big no-collateral loan anyway, don't land a job in that country's post-study window, and return to an Indian salary with an EMI in dollars
worst_case.mitigation: Before borrowing, price the worst case with the affordability calculator: loan size vs realistic first-year home-country salary. If the EMI eats >40% of it, that loan is a trap, not a bridge.
